text:
Abstract
Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful training paradigm
for LLM-based agents. However, scaling agentic RL for deep research re-
mains constrained by two coupled challenges: hand-crafted synthetic data
fails to elicit genuine real-world search capabilities, and real-world search
dependency during RL training introduces instability and prohibitive cost,
which limits the scalability of Agentic RL. LiteResearcher is a training
framework that makes Agentic RL scalable: by constructing a lite virtual
world that mirrors real-world search dynamics, we enable a continuously
improving training recipe that empowers a tiny search agent to outperform
large-scale open-source and commercial models (e.g., Tongyi DeepResearch
and Claude-4.5 Sonnet). Specifically, on common benchmarks such as GAIA
and Xbench, our LiteResearcher-4B achieves open-source state-of-the-art
results of 71.3% and 78.0% respectively, demonstrating that scalable RL
training is a key enabler for Deep Research Agents.

1
Introduction
Recent advances in reinforcement learning (RL) on reasoning models (Liu et al., 2025b;
Guo et al., 2025; Team et al., 2025a) have demonstrated that continuously scaling model
capabilities is achievable within stable training environments and with data of appropriate
difficulty. However, when transitioning to agentic RL, where reasoning must be integrated
with tool use, prior efforts have struggled to achieve similar sustained improvements.
We identify the primary bottleneck as the inherent noise and instability of the real-world
environment. In the domain of Coding Agents (Wei et al., 2025b; Jain et al., 2025), this
challenge has been effectively addressed through the use of isolated sandboxes configured
to simulate production environments while filtering out infrastructure-level noise, thereby
enabling scalable training.
However, existing works in the domain of Deep Research have yet to realize such scalable
RL training. Online RL frameworks (Zheng et al., 2025; Li et al., 2025d) interact directly
with the live internet, introducing significant training variance and prohibitive costs that
limit continuous capability growth. Local retrieval-based systems (Jin et al., 2025; Li et al.,
2025c; Sun et al., 2025) are typically confined to a narrow corpus, making it impossible to
replicate the complex search dynamics of the diverse internet. Meanwhile, current real-web
graph-based data synthesis methods (Jin et al., 2025; Li et al., 2025a; Gao et al., 2025) rely
on over-engineered reasoning structures, focusing on handcrafted logic while ignoring the
diversity of atomic search techniques required for deep research, such as cross-verification
and enumeration.
We argue that scaling agentic RL for Deep Research requires a virtual world that mirrors the
structure of the live internet but is isolated in execution: the twin architecture ensures that
policies optimized in the virtual environment can generalize to the open web, while isolation
shields the training process from the high variance and stochasticity of environmental noise.
In this paper, we present LiteResearcher, an Agentic RL training framework designed to
construct a virtual world that mirrors real-world web architecture. We follow these steps
to realize the scaling potential of agentic RL: (1) Synthesize real search data; (2) Simulate
search environment; (3) Achieve sustained improvements through curriculum RL.
The foundation of this virtual world is generating realistic training tasks. To capture
the diversity of information seeking in real-world user queries, we prioritize the scale of
information sources in our virtual world. We find that by scaling up information sources,
even a simple-but-effective data synthesis can yield a realistic distribution of training tasks.
Crucially, handling these synthetic tasks requires a corresponding execution environment.
We simulate a realistic search environment by enriching our corpus with these tasks. For
every synthetic task, we perform a targeted expansion of the local webpage corpus by
searching and fetching related real-world webpages, which then construct the local search
and browse tool. This ensures that for any given training task, the local search and browse
tools provide search dynamics analogous to the open web, allowing the agent to obtain
real-web atom capabilities in a controlled environment.
Even with synthetic tasks and tools, training can still saturate if the model gets trapped at a
certain difficulty threshold, correctly solving easy tasks while completely failing complex
ones. We apply a difficulty-aware curriculum RL which progressively enhances both task
difficulty and context length across training stages. At each stage, we only keep the synthetic
tasks that the current model can partially solve, discarding both trivially easy and impossibly
hard instances, thereby providing consistent training signal for improvement.
Our tiny search agent LiteResearcher-4B achieves 71.3% on GAIA (Mialon et al., 2023) and
78.0% on Xbench (Chen et al., 2025), surpassing open-source agents up to 8× larger such as
Tongyi DeepResearch 30B (70.9%/75.0%) and WebSailor 30B (53.2%/53.3%), while matching
or exceeding proprietary systems including Claude-4.5-Sonnet (71.2%/66.0%) and GLM-4.6
(71.9%/70.0%). These results demonstrate that scalable RL can yield a powerful on-device
deep research agent.
